title: Getting started
guided:
## Getting started

The first time you open Console, a short setup walks you from "I have a project an AI tool built for me" to a mapped, connected home base you can ship from. Every step is optional or changeable later, and nothing leaves your machine.

## What it does

Setup is a guided sequence of small steps. You start by telling Console whether you are new to this or already comfortable, and it adjusts how much it explains. Then it learns a few things about you and your project:

- **Your tools** - the AI coding tools you use (like Cursor, Claude, or an `AGENTS.md`-based agent). Console uses this so that when it suggests a fix, it hands the fix to the tool you actually use instead of doing the edit itself.
- **AI** - how Console should run AI when it answers questions about your project. You can paste an API key (a secret password that lets Console talk to an AI provider as you), run a fully offline local model, or wait for the hosted option.
- **Your project** - you point Console at a project folder on your computer. It reads that folder, with your permission, to learn what the project is, how it runs, and what services it connects to.
- **Goals** - what you want to do next (understand, ship, test, monitor, or harden), so Console can suggest the right next steps.
- **Connect** - linking services like GitHub and your host so you can act on the project, not just read about it.

When the read finishes, Console shows you a Report: a one-page summary of your project, scored by how completely it could map it. That Report becomes your starting screen, the Overview.

## How to use it

1. Choose **I'm new to this** on the first screen so Console explains each term as you go. You can switch modes any time later.
2. Step through the short feature tour, or click **Skip tour** to jump in.
3. On the sign-in step, click **Continue with GitHub** to sign in, or click **skip and stay local**. Signing in is optional.
4. Pick the AI coding tools you use, then choose how Console should run AI. If you pick **Your own API key**, select a provider, paste the key, and click **Connect**.
5. Click **Pick a folder** and select your project folder. Console reads it read-only on the next step.
6. Choose whether Console may read your `.env` file. Allowing it lets Console spot services like Stripe or Sentry. It reads only the key names, never the secret values.
7. Wait for **Studying your project** to finish. It moves to the Report on its own.
8. Read the Report, click **Looks good**, pick your goals, link any services you want, then click **Open the Overview**.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] "Not much to read here yet" on the Report
> This means the folder you picked is empty or is not a code project. Click **Back** and pick the folder that actually holds your project's code (the one with files like `package.json`).

> [!TIP] A low health score is not a grade on your code
> The health score measures how completely Console could read your project, not how good the code is. A low number usually means a missing `README`, license, or CI setup. The Repo panel can walk you through adding those.

> [!NOTE] Services not showing up
> If Console did not detect the services you use, you likely skipped the `.env` step. Open Settings and allow `.env` key-name reading, or link each service by hand from the Services panel.

## Reference

The Report shows a health ring whose colour reflects how completely Console mapped the project:

- **Green** - 80 or above, mapped well.
- **Amber** - 50 to 79, partly mapped, some metadata missing.
- **Red** - below 50, little could be read.

The setup steps, in order: Your tools, AI, Your project (pick folder, `.env` permission, and read), Goals, Connect. As a newcomer you also get a feature tour and a GitHub sign-in step at the start.
operator:
## Getting started

First-run setup takes you from a folder on disk to a mapped Overview you can ship from. It reads locally, persists your choices, and every step is skippable or editable later.

## What it does

A persona pick (**I know my way around** vs **I'm new to this**) sets explanation density, not the flow. Operator mode drops the tour and the standalone sign-in and starts at tool selection. The rest: dev-tool targets, AI runtime, a project root, an `.env` read consent, a local read, a profile Report, goals, and service links. Everything stays on the machine; keys are stored encrypted and never echoed.

## How to use it

1. Pick **I know my way around** to land directly on the tools step with the shorter stepper (no Account milestone).
2. Select your coding agents. This sets the handoff target Console writes to (`.cursor/rules`, `AGENTS.md`, `.claude`) when it ships a fix prompt.
3. Choose AI: **Your own API key** (OpenAI / Anthropic / Google, validated on save), **Local model** (Ollama, click Detect), or wait for hosted cloud.
4. **Pick a folder** for the project root, then allow or skip `.env` key-name detection. Key names only; values are never read or sent.
5. Let the read complete (it auto-advances to the Report), confirm with **Looks good**, set goals, link services, then **Open the Overview**.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] Report renders "Not much to read here yet"
> The root resolved to an empty dir or a non-code project. Go **Back** and repick the actual project root (the one with `package.json` / lockfile / source).

> [!TIP] Health score is read-confidence, not code quality
> The ring scores how completely Console mapped the project. Low usually means missing `README`, license, or CI workflow, surfaced for fix in the Repo panel, not bad code.

> [!NOTE] Detected services empty
> You skipped the `.env` consent, so providers were inferred from dependencies and config only. Flip `.env` key-name reading in Settings, or Link providers manually in Services.

## Reference

Health ring colour, by score: green at 80+, amber 50-79, red below 50.

AI providers accepted at setup: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google (BYO key), or local Ollama.

Stepper milestones (Operator): Your tools, AI, Your project, Goals, Connect. Guided adds an Account (GitHub sign-in) milestone and a feature tour ahead of these.

title: Overview
guided:
Overview is the first screen you see, and it answers one question in plain English: how is your project doing, and what is the one thing worth doing next.

## What it does

This page reads the live state of the project folder you have open and turns it into a short, calm summary you can act on. It pulls together a few things that usually live in separate places:

- **Is it ready to ship?** A "release gate" is a check that has to pass before your app is safe to release (tests green, build working, and so on). The page tells you, in words, whether you are clear to ship or whether something still needs your attention.
- **The numbers that matter.** Three small cards: **AI spend today** (what you have spent on AI through Console so far today), **Progress** (commits, the saved snapshots of your work, that have landed in the project), and **Tested** (test runs that finished in the last 24 hours).
- **Lately.** The most recent saved changes to your project, newest first, so you can see that work is actually moving.
- **Explore.** Three signposts to the rest of Console: the Workboard (what needs doing), reading the repo, and short walkthroughs.

Nothing here is something you fill in. Console works it all out from the folder you point it at. If a number is empty, it means that activity has not happened yet, not that something is broken.

## How to use it

1. Pick your project folder from the bar at the top of the window. Everything on this page is about that one folder.
2. Read the **Next step** line near the top. It is the single most useful thing to do right now. Click it to go straight there.
3. Check the **Is it ready to ship?** card to see whether your app can be released yet. Click it to open the full Release checklist and clear whatever is left.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] The page says "Pick a folder from the top bar"
> No project folder is open yet, so Console has nothing to read. Use the folder picker in the top bar to choose the folder your app lives in, and the page will fill in.

> [!NOTE] "Lately" says no recent commits
> A commit is a saved snapshot of your work. If you see "No commits in the last 24h," it just means nothing has been saved recently. As soon as you (or an AI tool working in the folder) save changes, they appear here automatically.

> [!TIP] The numbers all read zero
> Zero AI spend, progress, and tests means no automation has run in the last 24 hours yet. This is normal for a brand-new project. Run a test or make a change and the cards start filling in.

## Reference

The "Is it ready to ship?" card shows one of these states:

- **Ready** (green) - every check is passing, you can cut a release.
- **Pending** (amber) - one or more checks are still waiting on review.
- **Blocked** (amber) - a check needs your input before you can ship.
- **Failing** (red) - one or more checks are failing; open Release to investigate.
operator:
Overview is the landing cockpit: it answers "can I ship?" at a glance, surfaces what needs attention, and gives you the project's vitals and live git feed without leaving the page.

## What it does

Reads the active project folder and renders a status-first summary. The header subtitle is the inferred project shape. An alert strip up top is either "All systems healthy" or "Needs attention" with the urgent items. A ship verdict + release-gate donut answer the release question; a vitals row carries Build, Cost, Tests, Evals, and Deployment; below that, the next-action queue and the real recent-commit feed. All of it is derived, not configured.

## How to use it

1. Select the project folder in the top bar; the whole page scopes to it.
2. Scan the alert strip and ship card. The ship button reads `Ship release` when clear, `Open Release` otherwise, and both deep-link to the Release checklist.
3. Click any vital cell (or an alert item) to jump to its panel: Tests/Cost/Evals/Deployment to Observability or Services, Build to Pipeline, gates to Release.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] Recent activity reads "No commits found. This isn't a git checkout, or git isn't on PATH"
> The folder isn't a git repo, or `git` isn't resolvable. Open a git-tracked folder, or put `git` on PATH; the feed and the Progress vital both depend on it.

> [!NOTE] The Deployment vital is missing entirely
> That cell only mounts when Vercel is connected. Connect Vercel in Services to get it, and note it scopes to deploys Console made for this project, so a never-deployed project shows nothing rather than an unrelated URL.

> [!TIP] Cost today reads "Not tracked" instead of a dollar figure
> AI spend tracking isn't populated yet for this project. The figure appears once cost data is recorded; until then the cell is informational, not an error.

## Reference

Vitals row (each links out):

- **Tests** - runs in the last 24h, with the error count if any.
- **Build** - latest CI status; `idle` when no CI runs exist.
- **Cost today** - AI spend in USD, or "Not tracked".
- **Evals** - pass rate percent plus pass/fail/err and SBOM component count, or "No runs".
- **Deployment** - Vercel state badge (READY / BUILDING / ERROR / CANCELED) and live URL; shown only when Vercel is connected.

When both Tests and Evals have zero runs, those two cells collapse into one "Run a test" prompt. The release-gate donut center reads `{passing}/{total}` over three legend rows: passing (green), blocked (amber, includes failing gates), pending (slate).

title: Report
guided:
## A plain-English read of your project

The Report tells you what your project actually is, what it is built with, and how healthy it is, all read straight from your files. It is the page to open first when you inherit something an AI tool generated and you are not yet sure what you are holding.

## What it does

When you open a project folder, Console reads through its files and writes you a one-page summary. You do not have to understand the code first; the page does the first read for you.

At the top you get a **health score** out of 100. Think of it as a quick "is this in good shape" gauge: green means healthy, amber means it needs some work, red means it is at risk. Next to it sits a plain-language description of what the project is.

Below that, the page answers the questions a newcomer asks first:

- **What this is about** - a short summary of the project's purpose, taken from its own files (its README and config).
- **How to run** - the command that starts the project, so you are not guessing.
- **Built with** - one sentence naming the language and the main tools, plus a colour bar showing the language mix.
- **Services** - the outside connections the project uses, like a database or a sign-in provider.
- **Health** - a short list of the basic checks that are not passing yet, so you know what to shore up.

You can also click **Read deeper with AI**. That hands the project to a connected AI model, which reads it more closely and adds a richer description, a "What to fix first" list, and a plain run guide. This step is optional, and it only works once you have connected an AI provider.

## How to use it

1. Open the project folder you want to understand. The Report studies whatever project you have open.
2. Read the health score and the description at the top to learn what you are holding.
3. Look at **How to run** and copy the start command to get the project running on your machine.
4. Read **Health** to see what basic checks are failing, and treat that list as your first to-do list.
5. Click **Read deeper with AI** for a closer read and a "What to fix first" list. Connect an AI provider first if Console asks you to.
6. Click **Re-study** after you change files, so the page reflects the project as it is now.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] The page says "No project open"
> The Report only studies a project you have open. Pick a project folder first, then return to this page.

> [!NOTE] "Read deeper with AI" does nothing or shows an error
> The deeper read needs an AI provider connected. Open the Settings or Providers panel, connect a provider, then click the button again.

> [!TIP] The summary looks thin or out of date
> The plain summary comes from the project's own README and config files. If those are sparse, the summary will be too. Add a short README, or click **Re-study** after you make changes so the page re-reads your files.

## Reference

- **Green health (80-100)** - Healthy.
- **Amber health (50-79)** - Needs work.
- **Red health (0-49)** - At risk.
- **Services badge "in use"** - Console is confident this connection is wired in.
- **Services badge "maybe"** - Console found a hint of it but is not sure.
operator:
## A read-only profile of the open project, from its files

The Report profiles whatever project you have open: stack, services, health, and how to run it, derived from the source on disk. Open it first on an unfamiliar repo to get oriented before you read code.

## What it does

A one-pass scan of the open project, surfaced as a single page. A 0-100 health score (green >=80, amber 50-79, red <50), an identity line, a stat strip (file count, total LOC, package count, commit count + contributors), a language-share bar, detected services with high/low confidence, and a list of health signals.

"What this is about" is a deterministic summary from the README and config. **Read deeper with AI** / **Re-read with AI** hands it to a connected model and adds a tagline, narrative, a "What to fix first" list with evidence paths, a run guide, and per-package one-liners. It requires a connected provider.

Operator mode adds the full cockpit: tech-stack breakdown (frontend/backend/runtimes/build tools/package manager/data layer/API style/testing), services with evidence, AI tooling (MCP servers from `.mcp.json`, AI SDKs, eval frameworks, agent configs), CI provider and workflows, container base images, risk hotspots, and TODO/tag/CHANGELOG markers.

## How to use it

1. Open the target project. The Report profiles the active project root.
2. Scan the health score and signals to find what is failing the basic checks.
3. Pull the start command from **How to run** to get it booting locally.
4. Click **Read deeper with AI** for the narrative + "What to fix first" list; connect a provider first if prompted.
5. Click **Re-study** after edits to re-profile from the current tree.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] "Could not study this project"
> Console could not read the folder as a project. Confirm you opened a real project root (with a manifest like `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, or a `.git`), not an empty or nested directory, then click **Try again**.

> [!NOTE] AI sections never populate
> The narrative, "What to fix first", run guide, and per-package notes only appear after a successful AI read. Connect a provider, then click **Read deeper with AI**; the page surfaces the enrichment error inline if the call fails.

> [!TIP] Stack or health looks stale
> Every field is a snapshot from the last scan. After dependency or config changes, click **Re-study** to re-derive the profile, or **Re-read with AI** to refresh the narrative.

## Reference

- **Health 80-100** - green, Healthy.
- **Health 50-79** - amber, Needs work.
- **Health 0-49** - red, At risk.
- **Service confidence high** - badge reads "in use".
- **Service confidence low** - badge reads "maybe".
- **Re-study** - re-profiles the project from files only.
- **Read deeper / Re-read with AI** - runs the model enrichment pass (requires a provider).

title: Workboard
guided:
## Workboard

The Workboard is your one place to see everything still standing between you and shipping, sorted so the things that block you sit at the top.

## What it does

The Workboard pulls the open issues from your project's GitHub repository (the online home of your code) and lays them out in three stacked buckets:

- **Critical** - things that block shipping. Look at these first.
- **High** - important, but not blocking you today.
- **Other** - everything else, with the most recently updated items first.

An "issue" is just a written note in GitHub describing something to fix or build. Console reads a label on each issue (a small tag like `severity:critical`) to decide which bucket it lands in. Issues with no severity tag fall into **Other**.

Click any row to read the full issue, including its description and any comments, without leaving Console. There is also a **Slack** tab if your team reports problems in Slack instead of GitHub.

This view is read-only and built to be calm. When you are ready for the denser drag-and-drop board with more columns, the link at the bottom takes you there.

## How to use it

1. Open the Workboard. It loads the open issues from your GitHub repository automatically.
2. Read the **Critical** bucket first. These are the things stopping you from shipping.
3. Click a row to open it. Read the description and comments to understand what is being asked.
4. When you have read all of Critical, move down to **High**, then **Other**.
5. In a long bucket, click **Show N more** at the bottom to see the items that were folded away.

To read issues your team posted in Slack instead:

1. Click the **Slack** tab at the top.
2. If you see **Connect Slack to pull issues**, click **Open Services** and add your Slack token there first.
3. Once connected, reports appear grouped into Tech, Non-tech, and Test.

## Common pitfalls

> [!NOTE] The board is empty and that is fine
> If you see "Nothing to triage right now", your repository simply has no open issues. Many teams plan their work in pull requests instead of filing issues, so an empty board is normal, not a sign something broke.

> [!WARNING] "gh CLI not available"
> Console reads your issues through a small Git tool called `gh`. If it is missing, install it from cli.github.com, then run `gh auth login` in a terminal to sign in to GitHub. The board fills in once you do.

> [!WARNING] Slack tab says "Connect Slack"
> The Slack tab stays empty until you add a Slack token. Click **Open Services**, paste your Slack bot token, and tag the channels you want to watch as Tech, Non-tech, or Test. Their reports then show up here.

## Reference

Bucket dots, from most to least urgent:

- Red dot - **Critical**, blocks shipping
- Amber dot - **High**, important but not blocking today
- Hollow ring - **Other**, everything else, newest first
operator:
## Workboard

One view of every open issue between you and a ship, bucketed by severity so the blockers surface first.

## What it does

Reads open issues from your repo via `gh` and maps each onto a severity column from its `severity:*` label (`phase:*` labels land in Phase trackers; unlabelled issues fall to Other). Kanban and Table views over the same set. A Slack source tab groups pulled Slack reports by Tech / Non-tech / Test instead.

## How to use it

1. Open the Workboard. It lists up to 100 open issues from the repo named in the header.
2. Triage left to right: **Critical**, **High**, **Medium**, **Low**, **Phase trackers**, **Other**. Phase and Other collapse to narrow columns until you hover them.
3. Click any card to open the issue in-app (body + comments, rendered markdown) with **View on GitHub** in the footer.
4. To re-triage, drag a card to another severity column, or use the card's three-dot **Move to** menu. Either path edits the issue's `severity:*` label live (`gh issue edit --add/--remove-label`); the move reverts and toasts on failure.
5. Filter by title, `#num`, or label name with the filter box. Switch to **Table** for a flat sortless list with author, comment count, and updated date.

Slack source: click **Slack**, then triage Tech / Non-tech / Test. Rows link out via permalink.

## Common pitfalls

> [!NOTE] Empty board on a PR-first repo
> "No open issues" is a real state, not a fault. If you plan in PRs, the board stays empty by design; the empty state links to the repo's pull requests.

> [!WARNING] "gh CLI not available" / "gh not authenticated"
> The board is driven by `gh`. Install it from cli.github.com if missing, then run `gh auth login`. Until auth status is ok, columns stay empty.

> [!WARNING] Drag does nothing for Phase / Other
> Moving a card into or out of **Phase trackers** or **Other** touches no `severity:*` label, so it is a visual-only no-op (you get a "no severity label changed" toast). Only the four severity columns relabel the issue.

## Reference

Severity columns and their `severity:*` mapping:

- Red dot - **Critical** (`severity:critical`)
- Amber dot - **High** (`severity:high`)
- Hollow ring - **Medium** (`severity:medium`)
- Green dot - **Low** (`severity:low`)
- Small square - **Phase trackers** (`phase:*` labels; no relabel target)
- Grey dot - **Other** (no severity label; no relabel target)

Card chips: `area:*` labels render as chips (prefix stripped), up to four per card.

title: Architecture
guided:
## See how your project fits together

This page draws a map of your project so you can see, at a glance, which parts of it lean on which other parts. When an AI tool builds something for you, the pieces it writes are spread across many folders. This turns that sprawl into one picture you can read.

## What it does

Your project is made of **packages** (self-contained folders of code, each with a job) and the **dependencies** between them (one package using code from another). This page shows them as boxes joined by lines. A box is a package. A line means the package at the top of the line uses the one at the bottom.

There are two maps, and you switch between them with the **Code** and **Systems** buttons at the top right.

- **Code** is the literal map. Console reads your actual files, finds every place one package imports another, and draws those connections. It is always up to date with what is really in your folders.
- **Systems** is the plain-language map. A connected AI reads your project and groups it into named parts like "Frontend" or "Data layer", each tied to the real folders behind it. This one only appears after you ask Console to build it.

Below the Code map, packages are also grouped into simple buckets: **Shell** (the window that runs the app), **UI** (the screens you see), **Data** (the logic feeding those screens), and **Workspace** (your docs and build scripts). Each bucket has a one-line description so you can tell what each part is for without reading any code.

Click any box to open a panel that names what that package depends on and what depends on it, in words.

## How to use it

1. Pick your project folder from the bar at the top of the window. The map is blank until Console knows which folder to read.
2. Leave the view on **Code** to see the real connections Console found in your files.
3. Click any box to read, in plain English, what it depends on and what leans on it.
4. To get the named, plain-language map, click **Systems** at the top right, then click **Generate with AI**. Wait while it reads the project, then click any system to see its summary and the real files it covers.
5. In the Systems map, click a file path in the side panel to open that file.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] The map is empty and asks you to pick a folder
> Console has no project loaded yet. Open the folder picker in the bar at the top of the window and choose your project folder. The map fills in once a folder is selected.

> [!NOTE] The boxes are there but no lines connect them
> Your packages do not import one another, so there is nothing to draw a line for. This is normal for a small or brand-new project. The boxes alone still show you what parts exist.

> [!TIP] Systems says "No systems map yet"
> The plain-language map has not been built. Click **Generate with AI** on the Systems view. This needs a connected AI; if it reports an error, your AI connection is the thing to fix.

## Reference

The colored dot on each box in the Code map tells you what kind of code lives there:

- **Shell** - the desktop window that hosts the app.
- **Presentation** - screens and visible interface.
- **Domain** - core logic and rules.
- **Data** - storage and data access.
- **Infra** - build and plumbing.
- **Test** - test code.
- **External** - outside libraries your project pulls in (hidden by default).
operator:
## A live dependency graph of your project, plus an AI systems map

Two views of the same repo: **Code** is a package graph extracted from real imports; **Systems** is an AI-named semantic map onto validated repo paths. Pick a folder, read the structure, curate it if you want.

## What it does

**Code view**: Console walks your tree, extracts package-to-package import edges, and lays them out with a layered graph. Nodes carry tier, LOC, and file count. Click a node to highlight its in/out subgraph and open a sidebar listing exactly what it depends on and what depends on it. Cycles and truncation are flagged on-canvas.

**Systems view**: a connected AI groups the repo into named systems (frontend/backend/data/service/infra/shared/docs/tests), each mapped to real paths you can click open. Generated on demand, not extracted.

Curation (tier overrides, notes, hidden nodes, dragged positions) persists to `.refringence-console/architecture.json` and survives a recompute.

## How to use it

1. Select the project folder in the top bar.
2. Stay on **Code** and click a node to inspect its subgraph and edge list.
3. Click **Edit layout** to drag nodes; positions save automatically. Use **Reset layout** to hand the graph back to auto-layout (notes and tier overrides are kept).
4. Open a node's panel to override its **Tier**, add a **Note**, or **Hide** it from the canvas.
5. Toggle **All languages** (adds Python/Rust/Go/Java/Kotlin imports) or **External deps**, then **Recompute** to rebuild the graph with the wider scope.
6. Switch to **Systems**, click **Generate with AI**, then click any system to read its summary and open the files it maps to.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] "Could not read the repo summary"
> The folder hasn't been indexed yet. Hit **Retry**; if it persists, confirm the indexer has scanned this folder (the same scan that backs the Repo view).

> [!NOTE] Nodes render but no edges
> The packages don't import one another under the current extraction scope. Turn on **All languages** or **External deps** and **Recompute** to widen it. JS/TS-only is the default.

> [!TIP] "No systems map yet"
> Nothing has been generated. Click **Generate with AI** in Systems. It requires a connected AI; a failure surfaces the provider error inline, so fix the AI connection, not the graph.

## Reference

Code-view tiers (legend shows only tiers present on screen):

- **Shell** - app host / entry shell.
- **Presentation** - UI layer.
- **Domain** - core logic.
- **Data** - data access / storage.
- **Infra** - build and plumbing.
- **Test** - test code.
- **External** - third-party deps, dashed border, off by default, not hand-assignable.

Operator-only controls: **Edit layout**, **Reset layout**, **Recompute**, **All languages**, **External deps**, **N standalone** (show/hide packages with no detected edges), and per-node **Tier** / **Note** / **Hide**.

title: Pipeline and Release
guided:
See whether your project's automated checks are passing and whether this build is safe to ship, all in one place, before you push the button.

## What it does

This page has two parts that answer two questions about getting your app out the door.

**Pipeline** shows the automated jobs that run for you every time you push code. These jobs live in files inside your project at `.github/workflows/` (a folder GitHub watches). Console reads those files and draws each one as a left-to-right map: your code on the left (the **Source** node), the jobs in the middle in the order they run, and a **Deploy** node on the right if your project is set up to publish to Vercel or Netlify. When a job has run recently, a small coloured tag tells you if it passed, failed, or is still running.

**Release** answers one question: is this build ready to ship? It reads a checklist you keep in your project at `docs/release-checklists/<version>.yaml`, where each line is a safety "gate" (one thing that must be true before you ship, like "tests pass" or "no secrets leaked"). A donut chart and a plain headline sum it up: **Ready to ship**, **Awaiting checks** (something needs a human to confirm), or **Blocked from ship** (something is failing and must be fixed first).

A gate is just one check. A workflow is one automated job file. You read this page top to bottom: are my checks green, and can I ship?

## How to use it

1. Click **Pick a folder** and choose your project folder. Console scans it for workflow files and a release checklist.
2. On Pipeline, read each workflow map left to right to see what runs, in what order, after a push. Check the coloured tag next to each name to see if its last run passed.
3. On Release, read the headline and donut at the top. Green means ready. If it says **Blocked from ship**, click **Open blockers** to jump straight to the gates that are failing.
4. To run a workflow yourself without pushing code, click **Trigger** on that workflow. (This needs the GitHub command-line tool installed; if it is greyed out, that tool or your CI is not available.)

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] Pipeline says "No workflows found yet"
> Your project has no automation files yet. Add a file at `.github/workflows/<name>.yml` to your repo, and its steps will appear here as a map.

> [!WARNING] Release says "No release checklists yet"
> There is no checklist for Console to read. Create one at `docs/release-checklists/<version>.yaml` listing your gates, and the ship verdict and gate list will fill in.

> [!NOTE] "Cut release" and "Run all checks" are greyed out
> These buttons are labelled **Planned** and are not connected yet. For now, run your checks from the command line and tag the release by hand. The page still tracks and shows you the readiness.

## Reference

Workflow run tags (Pipeline):
- **Passing** (green) - the last run finished successfully
- **Failing** (red) - the last run failed
- **Running** (amber) - a run is queued or in progress
- No tag - Console has no run result to show yet

Gate statuses (Release):
- **Passing** (green) - this check is good, nothing for you to do
- **Needs review** / **Pending** (amber) - a person must confirm it before you ship
- **Blocked** / **Failing** (red) - this check is failing; fix it before shipping
- **Waiting** / **Blocked** (grey) - waiting on something outside this build
operator:
A read-only ship-readiness surface: your CI graph from `.github/workflows/`, plus a gate checklist verdict from `docs/release-checklists/<version>.yaml`, side by side before you cut a tag.

## What it does

**Pipeline** parses each `.github/workflows/*.yml` in the active project and renders it as a DAG: a Source node (git push) on the left, jobs laid out in `needs:` dependency order, and a Deploy node on the right only when `vercel.json` or `netlify.toml` is present. A per-workflow pill reflects the latest run's conclusion. A header stamp shows freshness ("as of HH:MM"); if run status can't be fetched you get a "Live run status unavailable" line with the reason.

**Release** reads the checklist for the selected version and renders a ship verdict (**Ready to ship** / **Awaiting checks** / **Blocked from ship**), a passing/blocked/pending donut, and the gate list with per-gate artifact links. An Environment variables card lists the variable names found in `.env*` files at the project root (names only, never values).

## How to use it

1. **Pick a folder** to set the active project. Pipeline scans `.github/workflows/`; Release scans `docs/release-checklists/` and the root `.env*` files.
2. Read the workflow graphs for stage order and the latest-run pill. Click **Trigger** to dispatch a workflow via `gh workflow run` without pushing.
3. On Release, switch versions via the picker, read the verdict, and click **Open N blockers** to filter the gate list to the failing gates. Use a gate's **View** to open its artifact.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] "No workflows found yet"
> The active root has no `.github/workflows/*.yml`. Add one and re-scan; the graph builds from the parsed `needs:` chain.

> [!WARNING] Trigger is disabled
> No `gh` CLI on PATH, or the repo exposes no dispatchable CI workflow. Install/authenticate GitHub CLI and ensure the workflow has a `workflow_dispatch` trigger.

> [!NOTE] "Cut release" and "Run all checks" are Planned
> Both are intentionally disabled. Run the QA harness from the CLI and cut the tag manually; this page tracks state, it doesn't execute it yet.

## Reference

Workflow run pills (Pipeline) - from the latest run:
- **Passing** - conclusion `success`
- **Failing** - conclusion `failure`
- **Running** - status `in_progress` or `queued`, no conclusion yet
- no pill - anything else (unclassifiable / no run data)

Gate statuses (Release):
- **Passing** (green) - gate clear
- **Pending** / **Needs review** (amber) - awaiting human sign-off
- **Failing** / **Blocked** (red) - failing, counts as a blocker
- **Blocked** / **Waiting** (grey) - waiting on an external dependency

The donut and counts split gates as passing (green) / blocked (red+blocked) / pending (amber); the verdict is `Blocked` if any red or blocked, else `Awaiting checks` if any amber, else `Ready to ship`.

title: Services, connections and deploy
guided:
## Services, connections and deploy

This page is where you plug your project into the outside world: link your code host, your error tracker, your chat, and then push your app live to the web without leaving Console.

## What it does

Most apps you build need a few outside services to actually go live. Your code lives on a host (GitHub). Your app runs on the internet somewhere (Vercel). When it breaks, an error tracker (Sentry) tells you. This page is one screen where you connect those accounts to Console once, so the rest of the app, your Workboard, your releases, your usage numbers, can show you real data instead of blanks.

Each service shows up as a card. A card tells you whether it is **Connected** (green tick) or **Not connected**, and gives you a button to do something about it. Once GitHub and Vercel are both connected, you can deploy the project you have open straight from the Vercel card.

A few things connect differently:

- **GitHub** connects by reusing the login you already have on your computer through the GitHub command-line tool (`gh`). There is no password or token to paste.
- **Vercel**, **Sentry**, **Slack**, and the providers under **Usage and connectors** need an access token (a long secret string you copy from that provider's website). You paste it once. Console encrypts it using your operating system's built-in keychain (the secure store Windows or macOS uses for passwords) and never shows it again or sends it to the AI.

There is also a **From your .env** section. A `.env` file is a small text file in your project that lists secret keys your app needs. Console reads only the NAMES in that file (never the secret values) and offers to link any service it recognises with one tap.

## How to use it

**Connect GitHub (do this first)**

1. Find the **GitHub** card, or the blue "Connect GitHub to get started" banner at the top.
2. Click **Connect GitHub**.
3. Wait for the card to show **Connected** with your username. If it fails, you need to sign in to GitHub on your computer first (see pitfalls below).

**Connect Vercel and deploy your app live**

1. Find the **Vercel** card and click **Connect**.
2. In the dialog, click **Create a token**, sign in to Vercel, copy the token it gives you, and paste it into the **Access token** box.
3. Click **Connect**. The card now shows your Vercel username.
4. Make sure you have a project open in Console. Then, on the Vercel card, click **Deploy**.
5. Check the name in the dialog, then click **Deploy to production**. The status moves from `Uploading` to `Building` to `READY`, and a clickable link to your live site appears.

**Link a service from your .env file**

1. Open a project that has a `.env` file.
2. Look for the **From your .env** section. Each service Console recognised shows its key name.
3. Click **Link** next to it. Console reads the value just long enough to test and store the connection, then drops it.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] GitHub says it could not connect
> Console reuses your existing GitHub command-line login. If you have never signed in, there is nothing to reuse. Open a terminal, run `gh auth login`, finish the sign-in, then click **Connect GitHub** again.

> [!WARNING] The Deploy button is greyed out
> Deploy only works when a project is open. If the button is disabled or you see "Open a project first", open your project folder in Console, then return to the Vercel card and click **Deploy**.

> [!NOTE] The "From your .env" section is missing
> That section only appears when you have a project open and its `.env` file lists a service Console knows. No open project, no `.env` file, or no recognised keys means the section stays hidden. Open the right project, or connect the service manually from its card instead.

> [!TIP] Slack channels are empty when you map them
> Console can only see Slack channels your bot has been invited into. If "Map channels" shows no channels, go to Slack and invite the bot to the channels you want watched, then reopen the dialog.

## Reference

Connection status on a card:

- **Connected** (green tick) - the account is linked and working.
- **Not connected** - no account linked yet.
- **Checking...** - Console is still confirming; wait a moment.
- **Couldn't check connections** - Console could not reach the connection store this time; reopen the page or retry.

Vercel deploy status, in order: `Uploading`, `Building`, `READY` (live, link shown), or `ERROR` / `CANCELED` (failed).
operator:
## Services, connections and deploy

Link your host, error tracker, chat, and analytics to Console once, then deploy the open project to production from the Vercel card. Real connection state, not cosmetic badges.

## What it does

One screen of provider cards backing the Workboard, releases, and usage tiles with live data. GitHub reuses local `gh` auth (no token). Vercel, Sentry, Slack, and the Usage-and-connectors providers (OpenRouter, ElevenLabs, Netlify, Supabase, Neon, Railway, PostHog) take a pasted token, encrypted in the OS keychain, never echoed back, never sent to the model. A **From your .env** block scans key NAMES in the open project's `.env` and one-click links what it recognises (value read transiently, then dropped).

## How to use it

**GitHub + Vercel + deploy**

1. Click **Connect GitHub** on the card or hero banner. It links via existing `gh` auth and shows your login.
2. On the **Vercel** card, click **Connect**, paste a Vercel access token (Create a token link opens `vercel.com/account/tokens`), confirm.
3. Open the project you want shipped, then click **Deploy** on the Vercel card.
4. Review the suggested name and detected framework/output, click **Deploy to production**. Status polls `Uploading` -> `Building` -> `READY`; the production URL becomes a clickable link. Zero-config: no `vercel.json`, static sites detected automatically.

**Connect a token-based provider (Sentry / Slack / connectors)**

1. Click **Connect** on the card; paste the token. Sentry also needs the org slug. Slack needs an `xoxb-` bot token with `channels:read` + `channels:history` (`groups:history` for private).
2. For Slack, click **Map channels** and tag each channel as `Tech`, `Non-tech`, `Test`, or `Not watched` to pull it into the Workboard.

**Link from .env**

1. Open a project with a `.env`. The **From your .env** section lists recognised services by key name.
2. Click **Link**. The value is read once to validate and store, then discarded.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] GitHub connect fails
> No local `gh` session to reuse. Run `gh auth login`, then retry Connect.

> [!WARNING] Deploy disabled / "Open a project first"
> The Vercel deploy flow targets the active project. With no project open, Deploy is disabled. Open the project, then deploy.

> [!NOTE] "From your .env" section absent
> Shown only with a project open AND a `.env` whose key names match a known service. No project, no `.env`, or no recognised keys hides it. Connect from the provider card instead.

> [!TIP] Map channels shows nothing
> The bot only sees channels it has joined. Invite the bot to the target channels in Slack, then reopen Map channels.

## Reference

Card status badges:

- **Connected** - linked and verified.
- **Not connected** - no credential stored.
- **Checking...** - connection query in flight.
- **Couldn't check connections** - connection store unreachable this load; retry.

Vercel deploy states: `Uploading` -> `Building` -> `READY` (URL shown) | `ERROR` | `CANCELED`.

Slack channel teams: `Tech`, `Non-tech`, `Test`, `Not watched`.

title: Observability
guided:
Run your project's tests and checks from inside Console, then see at a glance whether your last run passed, where the report files live, and whether anything is breaking for real users.

## What it does

This page is your "is it working?" dashboard. Instead of opening a terminal and remembering commands, you press a button to run a test, and Console shows you the result in plain words.

A **test run** is one round of automatic checks against your project. Some catch broken screens, some check that your app starts at all. When a run finishes, it leaves behind a folder of **report files** (logs, screenshots, results). Console keeps a list of these runs so you can look back at them.

You will see a few things on this page:

- A button to **run a test** and watch it happen live.
- A **last run** card that tells you, in one sentence, whether your most recent run passed, failed, or was unclear, with a button to open its report folder.
- A list of your **recent runs**, each marked **Passing**, **Failed**, or **Unclear**.

"Unclear" means the run finished but did not leave enough behind for Console to be sure either way. It is not a failure on its own, just a "look closer."

If you switch to **Operator view** (top right), you get more: buttons for different kinds of test, a tally of eval results, counters, and a feed of live errors from real users if you have connected an error service.

## How to use it

1. Press **Run a test**. Console starts the check and shows "Your test is running. This can take a minute."
2. Wait for it to finish. A small message tells you when it is done.
3. Read the **Last run** card. It will say your run finished cleanly, had a number of problems, or was unclear.
4. Press **Open last test report** (or **See the report folder**) to open the run's folder on your computer and read the details.

## Common pitfalls

> [!NOTE] No test runs yet
> If the page says "No test runs yet," nothing has been recorded for this project. Press **Run a test** to create your first one, or in a terminal at your project folder run `npm run smoke`.

> [!WARNING] The result says "Unclear"
> An Unclear result means the run finished but left no clear pass-or-fail signal behind. Open the report folder with **See the report folder** and read the files inside, or run the test again to get a cleaner result.

> [!TIP] The report folder will not open
> If clicking open shows an error, the run folder may have been deleted or moved. Run a fresh test to create a new report folder, then open that one.

## Reference

Status labels on each run:

- **Passing** - the run finished and none of its files signalled a failure.
- **Failed** - at least one file in the run signalled a failure or error.
- **Unclear** - the run finished but left nothing Console can read as pass or fail.
operator:
Launch your project's test, eval, e2e, and CI commands from one surface, watch them stream live, and triage what they leave behind: a runs table, eval pass/fail tallies, run counters, and production errors from Sentry.

## What it does

A trigger-and-triage console over your QA runs. The Run buttons spawn real commands in your project root and stream stdout into a live console; finished runs land in a runs table with status, file count, and size. Operator view adds the eval summary, counters, and the Sentry feed; Guided view collapses to a single Run a test button plus a last-run card.

Run status is read from a run manifest when present; otherwise it is inferred from artifact filenames (an `inferred` tag flags that case).

## How to use it

1. Click **Run smoke**, **Run e2e**, **Run evals**, or **Run CI**. e2e, evals, and CI disable themselves when the project has no tests, no eval config, or no workflow under `.github/workflows` - the tooltip names which.
2. Watch the live console; use the stop control on the running indicator to kill it. On completion you get a toast and the runs table refreshes.
3. Expand any row in the **Runs** table to list its artifact files, then **Open** a file or **Open folder** from the artifact log to inspect on disk.
4. For real-user errors, read the **Production errors (Sentry)** section; **Open** jumps to the issue permalink.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] Run e2e / Run evals / Run CI is greyed out
> The button is disabled because the project is missing what it needs: tests for e2e, an eval config for evals, or a workflow file under `.github/workflows` for CI. Add the missing piece (or run `Run smoke`, which is always available) and the button enables.

> [!NOTE] Runs table is empty or hides your run
> Runs with zero files or zero bytes are filtered out of the main table and tucked under **Show empty runs (N)**. If a run you expected is missing, it produced no artifacts - check the command output. With nothing recorded at all, run `npm run smoke` or `npm run eval:regression` from the repo root.

> [!TIP] Production errors says "Connect Sentry in Services"
> The Sentry feed is empty until you connect Sentry on the Services page. Connect it there; unresolved issues from the last 24h then populate this section.

## Reference

Runs table status pills:

- **ok** - run has files and no filename signals failure or error.
- **failed** - an artifact filename matches fail/error.
- **empty** - run has no artifact files.
- **inferred** tag - status came from filename heuristics, not a run manifest.

Run buttons and what they invoke:

- **Run evals** - `npm run eval:promptfoo`
- **Run e2e** - `npm run qa:e2e`
- **Run smoke** - `npm run smoke`
- **Run CI** - `gh workflow run <first workflow in .github/workflows>`

Counters: **Runs total**, **Runs / 24h**, **Errors total**, **Errors / 24h** (turns red when above zero).

title: Repo, Library and Prompts
guided:
Get your bearings on the project an AI built for you: see how to run it, what needs fixing before it can ship, read its docs like a book, and keep the prompts that get good results out of your coding assistant.

## What it does

This is three connected workspaces for the project folder you have open.

**Repo** is your map. When an AI tool hands you a folder of code, you often do not know what it is or how to start it. Repo reads the folder and tells you, in one place: the command that starts the app, whether the project is even runnable yet, which everyday files a healthy project should have (and which are missing), and a few safety checks. It never runs your code or changes it. It only looks and reports.

**Library** lets you read the project's own writing. Every README (a plain-text file that explains the project), every doc, and every config file is listed in a folder tree you can click through. Markdown files render as a clean page, like an article, with a "X min read" estimate. You read your project like a book instead of hunting through files.

**Prompts** is your saved-instructions drawer. A prompt is a block of text you paste into a coding assistant to tell it what to do. Good ones are worth keeping. Console ships a set of "Curated" prompts to start you off, and you can save your own. Each prompt can have blanks (called variables) that you fill in, then send the finished text straight to your AI.

## How to use it

**Find out how to run the project (Repo)**
1. Pick your project folder from the top bar if you have not already.
2. Open Repo. Look at the coloured dot near the top: green means Console found a start command, amber means it found code but no clear way to run it.
3. If there is a start command, find the "How to run it" box, click the copy button, and paste the command into your terminal.

**Get the project ready to ship (Repo)**
1. In Repo, read the "Project setup" card. It shows how many essentials are in place and lists what is missing (for example a README, a LICENSE, or a `.gitignore`).
2. Read "Dependency health" for outdated or known-vulnerable packages, and "Exposed secrets" for passwords or keys accidentally left in the code.
3. Click the small "Fix" button next to any problem to send it to your AI to repair.

**Reuse a prompt (Prompts)**
1. Open Prompts and click any prompt in the left list. The right side opens with its blanks.
2. Fill in each blank. The preview updates as you type.
3. Click **Copy** to copy the finished text, or **Send to AI** to drop it straight into Console's chat.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] Every panel is empty or says "Pick a project folder"
> Repo, Library, and Prompts all read from the one project folder you have open. If nothing shows, no folder is active. Pick your project folder from the top bar and the three panels fill in.

> [!NOTE] The dot is amber and says "no start command"
> Console found code but no declared way to start it, so it cannot show you a run command. Open the **Set up for Console** button in Repo, or ask your AI to add a start script to the project, then re-open Repo.

> [!TIP] A "Curated" prompt will not let you edit it
> Curated prompts are read-only on purpose so the originals stay intact. Use the clone button to make your own editable copy, then change that copy freely.

## Reference

Repo status dot:
- **Green, "runnable"** - Console found a command that starts the project.
- **Amber, "no start command"** - code is there, but no clear way to run it yet.
- **Grey, "reading"** - Console is still scanning the folder.
- **"CI"** appended - the project has automated checks set up under `.github/workflows`.

Prompt list filters:
- **Favorites** - only prompts you starred.
- **Mine** - only prompts you saved yourself.
- **Curated** - only the read-only prompts Console ships with.
- **Category and tag chips** - narrow to one topic.
operator:
Orientation for an AI-generated repo you did not write: how it runs, what blocks shipping, its docs rendered in place, and a prompt drawer wired to your dev tools. All three read the active project; none of them mutate or execute your code.

## What it does

**Repo** is a read-only orientation surface: detected start command, a runnable/no-start/reading status, project-setup hygiene (README, LICENSE, `.gitignore`, CI, lockfile, tests, `.env.example`), an `.env.example` vs `.env` key-name diff, dependency health (OSV advisories + newer npm releases), an in-source secret scan, and a structure view grouped by package role.

**Library** is a folder-tree reader over the repo's docs, READMEs, and configs. Markdown renders as a page with a read-time estimate; JSON/YAML/TOML are syntax-highlighted. Copy path or open in your editor per file.

**Prompts** is a per-project template library (search, category/tag/favorite/source filters), `{{var}}` interpolation with live preview, and a handoff bar that routes the filled text to copy, `.cursorrules`, `AGENTS.md`, `claude`, or Cursor.

## How to use it

**Run the project**
1. Set the active project in the top bar.
2. In Repo, read the status dot: green = start command found, amber = code but none declared.
3. Copy the command from the "How to run it" box and run it in your terminal.

**Clear ship blockers**
1. In Repo, scan Project setup, Dependency health, and Exposed secrets.
2. Hit the **Fix** affordance on a finding to hand it to the agent as a repair task.
3. Use **Set up for Console** to write a managed-block guideline into `AGENTS.md` or `.cursorrules` so the agent shapes the repo the way Console reads it.

**Fire a prompt at your dev tool**
1. Select a prompt; fill its variables (preview updates live).
2. **Copy** or **Send to AI** for the in-app chat.
3. Or use the handoff bar: **Run in Claude Code**, **Write AGENTS.md**, **Write .cursorrules**, **Open in Cursor**. Greyed buttons mean the tool was not found on `PATH`; file writes still work, launches do not.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] Panels are empty
> No active project. Repo, Library, and Prompts all key off the folder in the top bar - select it and they populate. Prompts are stored per project under `.refringence-console`, so a new project starts with the curated set only.

> [!NOTE] Secret scan looks clean but you have keys in `.env`
> The scan deliberately skips `.env` files and only flags hardcoded secrets in source and config. A green result means nothing leaked into committed code, not that you have no secrets. Keep real values in `.env` and document key names in `.env.example`; the env diff checks names only, never values.

> [!TIP] Curated prompt is read-only
> Built-ins do not edit in place. Clone to your library first, then edit the copy. Same for the dependency and outdated checks - they are read-only network lookups against OSV and npm and never install or upgrade anything.

## Reference

Repo status dot:
- Green / "runnable" - start command detected.
- Amber / "no start command" - codebase found, nothing declared.
- Grey / "reading" - scan in progress.
- "· CI" suffix - workflows present under `.github/workflows`.

Handoff bar targets (filled prompt): Copy, Write `.cursorrules`, Write `AGENTS.md`, Run in Claude Code (needs `claude` on PATH), Open in Cursor. Files truncate in Library at 256 KB - open in editor for the rest. Skills install per project into `.claude/skills` or `.codex/skills`; nothing global is touched.

title: AI Assistant
guided:
Ask questions about your own project and get answers that are based on your real files, not guesses. This is the chat where you tell the AI what you want to do next and it helps you do it.

## What it does

The Assistant is a chat. You type what you want in plain words, and the reply streams back line by line as it is written.

What makes it more useful than a generic chatbot is that it can open and read the files in the project you are working on. When it does, you will see small cards above the answer naming what it looked at, for example `read_file` or `search_code`. That is the Assistant grounding its answer in your actual code instead of making things up.

Two terms you will meet:

- A **provider** is the company or program that runs the AI. Console supports OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google (these run in the cloud and need a key), plus **Ollama** (this runs on your own machine and needs no key).
- A **key** (also called an API key) is a password-like string the cloud provider gives you so it knows the requests are yours. You paste it once in Settings; Console keeps it on this machine and never shows it back to you.

The Assistant opens as a panel on the right side of the window. One thing to know up front: the conversation is not saved. If you close Console or refresh, the chat clears and you start fresh.

## How to use it

**Connect a provider (do this once):**

1. Open **Settings** and find the **AI** section.
2. Under **Provider keys**, paste your key into OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google, then click **Set**. A green **Connected** badge means it worked. (If you would rather run locally, click **Detect Ollama** instead, no key needed.)
3. If the key is wrong you will see "That key did not check out." Re-copy it from the provider's website and try again.

**Ask a question:**

1. Open the Assistant from the **Assistant** button in the top bar.
2. Pick a model from the dropdown at the top. The list only shows models your connected provider can actually use.
3. Type your question in the box at the bottom and press **Enter** to send. Use **Shift+Enter** if you want a new line without sending.
4. Watch the answer stream in. Press the red **Stop** button if you want to cut it off early.

**Start over:**

1. Click **New chat** at the top to clear the conversation and begin a fresh one.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] The chat says "Connect an AI provider"
> No provider has a working key yet, so there is no model to talk to. Click **Open Settings**, paste a key under **Provider keys** and click **Set**, or click **Detect Ollama** to use a local model. The green **Connected** badge confirms you are ready.

> [!NOTE] The answer does not seem to know about your code
> The Assistant can only read files when a project is open. Open the project you want to ask about first, then ask again. When it is reading your files you will see cards like `read_file` appear above the reply.

> [!TIP] Your conversation vanished
> The chat is not saved between sessions, and **New chat** also clears it on purpose. If an answer matters, copy it out before you close Console or start a new chat.

## Reference

The model dropdown groups what you can use by provider:

- **OpenAI** - cloud, needs a key starting `sk-...`
- **Anthropic** - cloud, needs a key starting `sk-ant-...`
- **Google** - cloud, needs a key starting `AIza...`
- **Ollama** - runs on your machine, no key, click **Detect Ollama** to find it

In Settings, the connection badge reads either **Connected** (green) or **Not connected**.
operator:
A project-grounded chat: ask in natural language, get a streamed reply, and watch the model read your real files (`read_file`, `list_files`, `search_code`) instead of hallucinating against an empty context.

## What it does

Right-side Assistant dock (or the `/ai` page) over a multi-provider backend: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, plus local Ollama. The model picker offers only models reachable with your stored keys, so a selection never fails on send. With a project open, the chat gets read-only file tools and grounds answers in your code; tool calls render as cards above the reply. No persistence: the transcript lives in component state and clears on close, refresh, or **New chat**.

## How to use it

1. In **Settings -> AI -> Provider keys**, paste a key for OpenAI (`sk-...`), Anthropic (`sk-ant-...`), or Google (`AIza...`) and **Set**. Keys are validated on save and held in the app keystore, write-only. For local, **Detect Ollama** instead, no key.
2. Open the Assistant from the top bar, pick a model from the provider-grouped dropdown (shows context size and a one-line capability note).
3. Open the project you want grounded answers about so the file tools have a root.
4. Type, **Enter** to send (**Shift+Enter** for newline), **Stop** to cancel mid-stream, **New chat** to reset.

## Common pitfalls

> [!WARNING] Empty state: "Connect an AI provider"
> No keyed provider resolves to any usable model. Open Settings, **Set** a provider key (wait for the green **Connected** badge) or **Detect Ollama**. Note the gate is on *available models*, not raw key presence: a connected provider whose key reaches nothing still leaves the chat empty.

> [!NOTE] Answers ignore the codebase
> File tools only attach when a project is active. Open the project before asking; confirm grounding by the `read_file` / `search_code` tool cards above the reply.

> [!TIP] Transcript gone after reload
> Conversations are in-memory only and **New chat** discards them deliberately. Copy anything worth keeping before closing or resetting.

## Reference

Providers and key formats (Settings -> AI -> Provider keys):

- **OpenAI** - cloud, `sk-...`
- **Anthropic** - cloud, `sk-ant-...`
- **Google** - cloud, `AIza...`
- **Ollama** - local, no key, **Detect Ollama**

Settings AI tiers (where AI is allowed to run):

- **On-device only** - all requests via Ollama, nothing leaves the machine (default)
- **On-device with optional cloud** - local first, cloud per opt-in
- **Cloud-first** - every request goes to the cloud provider, requires a key

Save-key feedback: green **Connected** badge on success; "That key did not check out." on a rejected key.

title: Tool config
guided:
The Tool config panel shows what your AI coding tools are told to do, all in one place. If you use Cursor, Claude Code, or another AI tool on this project, each one keeps its own rules and settings files. This panel reads them and lays them out together, so you can see at a glance what each tool will and will not do.

## What it does

An AI coding tool follows a set of project files: a rules file that tells it your conventions, a permissions list of what it is allowed to do, and sometimes a list of extra tools it can call. Console reads those files for six tools (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, Codex, and Gemini) and shows each one's settings as a section you can open.

It only reads. It never changes your files. For privacy it reads only the NAMES of any environment variables, never their values, and nothing leaves your machine.

## How to use it

1. Open Tool config from the sidebar.
2. The tools that have config files in this project show at the top. Open one to read its rules, what it is allowed to do, and any extra tools it can use.
3. Tools with no config files here are listed under "Not configured here".
4. After you edit a config file, press Rescan to pick up the change.

## Common pitfalls

> [!NOTE] A tool says "not configured here"
> That just means the tool has no config files in this project yet. If you want to set up house rules for it, the Prompts panel can write an AGENTS.md or a .cursorrules file for you.
operator:
Tool config reads, deterministically and read-only, the per-project config each AI coding tool keeps, and shows them side by side: instructions, permissions, MCP servers, and hooks for Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Codex, and Gemini.

## What it does

One section per tool, present or absent. For Claude Code it surfaces the permissions table (deny / ask / allow), the MCP server list, hooks, and the agent, command, and skill instruction bodies. For the rules-based tools it shows each rule's globs / applyTo / trigger and its body. It reads only key NAMES for any env, never values, and only the enumerated config files, never your .env.

## How to use it

1. Open Tool config. Present tools render expanded with their file count; absent tools collapse to a chip row.
2. Expand a tool to read its permissions, MCP servers, hooks, and instruction docs.
3. Rescan after editing any config file to refresh.

## Reference

What each tool's section reads:

- Claude Code: CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json (and settings.local.json), .claude/commands, .claude/agents, .claude/skills
- Cursor: .cursor/rules/*.mdc, and the legacy .cursorrules
- Copilot: .github/copilot-instructions.md, .github/instructions/*.instructions.md
- Windsurf: .windsurf/rules, and the legacy .windsurfrules
- Codex: AGENTS.md
- Gemini: GEMINI.md, .gemini/settings.json

> [!TIP] Editing is owned by Prompts
> This panel is read-first. To write house rules, use the dev-tool handoff in Prompts, which manages an AGENTS.md or .cursorrules block.

title: Design system
guided:
The Design system panel shows what your project looks like, all in one place: its colours, fonts, text sizes, and the building-block libraries it uses. It is a quick way to see your whole design at a glance, and to compare it with another project later.

## What it does

Most web projects keep their colours and fonts in a few setup files. Console reads those files and draws the result for you: a grid of colour swatches, the set of text sizes (the "type scale"), the spacing and corner-rounding steps, and the names of any design libraries it found, like Tailwind or shadcn.

It only reads your files. It never changes them.

## How to use it

1. Open Design system from the sidebar.
2. Scroll the palette to see every colour with its name, and the type scale to see each text size rendered at its real size.
3. Press Save profile to keep a snapshot of this project's design.
4. Once you have saved two or more, the Compare section shows what changed between them: colours added or changed, text sizes, and libraries.

## Common pitfalls

> [!NOTE] It says "No design tokens found"
> The panel reads a project's design files (a Tailwind setup, a shadcn components.json, or CSS colour variables). If your project does not use those, there is nothing to show yet. The panel reads your design, it does not create one.
operator:
Design system reads, deterministically, the project's design language and renders it: the colour palette, fonts, type scale, spacing and radii, and the component libraries. It also saves a project's design as a cross-project profile and diffs two profiles.

## What it does

It statically parses a Tailwind v3 config (never executing it) or the Tailwind v4 CSS-first @theme blocks, reads a shadcn components.json, and walks the :root / .dark CSS token blocks, resolving var() chains so a token lands as its real value. Colours are kept as authored, so oklch renders natively in the swatches.

## How to use it

1. Open Design system. The header shows the detected stack, for example Tailwind v4, shadcn new-york.
2. Browse the palette, type scale, spacing and radii, and the detected libraries; the "Detected from" list shows exactly which files fed the result.
3. Save profile snapshots the current design to a store shared across projects.
4. With two or more saved, Compare diffs their palette, type steps, fonts, and library sets.

## Reference

What it detects:

- Tailwind: a tailwind.config.* (v3) or the v4 @theme / @theme inline / @custom-variant CSS blocks
- shadcn: components.json (style, base colour, css-variables)
- CSS tokens: :root, .dark, and [data-theme] custom-property blocks
- Libraries: from package.json dependencies (MUI, Chakra, Mantine, Radix, Ant Design, Tailwind, shadcn, and more)

> [!TIP] Profiles are portable
> A saved profile holds tokens only (no file contents or paths), so you can compare the design of two different projects.

title: Settings and shortcuts
guided:
Make Console look and behave the way you want, and move around it without reaching for the mouse. Settings holds your theme, how much hand-holding you get, and where your AI runs; the keyboard shortcuts let you jump to any screen in one keystroke.

## What it does

Settings is one screen with a few choices that stick between sessions.

- **Theme** is the colour scheme: Light, Dark, or System (System follows whatever your operating system is set to).
- **Mode** is how much Console explains itself. **Guided** shows extra explainers and roomier screens for when you are still learning. **Operator** hides them for a tighter, denser view once you know your way around. (This page you are reading has both.)
- **AI mode** is where the AI assistant runs. **On-device only** keeps everything on your laptop and sends nothing out. **On-device with cloud help** runs locally but asks before sending anything to a cloud service. **Cloud first** uses a cloud provider for every request and needs an API key (a secret password from that provider, like OpenAI or Anthropic).

Once you switch to the fuller view, Settings also lets you choose your active project folder (the folder Console is working on), reorder or hide items in the left sidebar, paste API keys, and check whether a local AI runner called Ollama is installed.

A **command palette** is a search box for the whole app. You press a shortcut, type a few letters, and it finds the screen, the action, or the help page you mean. You never have to remember where anything lives.

## How to use it

**Change your theme or mode:**

1. Open Settings from the left sidebar (the gear icon).
2. Pick **Light**, **Dark**, or **System** under Theme. The change happens at once.
3. Pick **Guided** or **Operator** under Mode.

**See every setting (keys, project folder, sidebar):**

1. In Settings, find the **About** card.
2. Click **See full settings**. Console switches to Operator mode and shows the left menu: Display, AI, Sidebar, Project, Services.

**Jump anywhere with the keyboard:**

1. Press `Ctrl` and `K` together to open the command palette.
2. Type the name of a screen, an action like "Deploy", or a help page.
3. Press `Enter` to go there. Press `Esc` to close without choosing.

**Use the quick jump chords:**

1. Make sure you are not typing in a text box.
2. Press `g`, then release it and press a single letter (for example `g` then `s` for Services).
3. Press `?` at any time to see the full list of these shortcuts.

## Common pitfalls

> [!NOTE] Most settings are hidden until you ask for them
> If you only see Theme and AI mode, you are in Guided mode. The API keys, project folder, and sidebar editor live behind the **See full settings** link in the About card. Click it to reveal them.

> [!WARNING] The keyboard chords do nothing while you are typing
> The `g`-then-letter jumps and the `?` help are switched off while your cursor is in a text box or the command palette is open, so they never eat your keystrokes. Click on empty space first, then press the chord.

> [!TIP] "Start over" keeps your keys and connections
> The **Start over** button clears your mode, your active project, and your first-run setup, then reopens the welcome flow. It does not delete saved API keys or connected accounts. It asks you to confirm with a second click, so a single tap will not wipe anything.

## Reference

Command palette and shortcuts:

- `Ctrl` + `K` - open the command palette
- `?` - show the keyboard shortcuts list
- `Esc` - close the palette
- `Enter` - go to the selected item
- `g` then `o` - Overview
- `g` then `w` - Workboard
- `g` then `r` - Repo
- `g` then `a` - Architecture
- `g` then `p` - Pipeline
- `g` then `s` - Services
- `g` then `b` - Library
- `g` then `l` - Release
- `g` then `y` - Observability
- `g` then `c` - Activity
- `g` then `t` - Walk through
- `g` then `d` - Docs
- `g` then `,` - Settings

Provider key status:

- **Connected** (green) - Console holds a working key for that provider
- **Not connected** - no key saved yet
operator:
Tune theme, persona, density, AI routing, and your active project, then navigate the whole app from the keyboard without touching the mouse.

## What it does

Settings persists theme, persona (Guided/Operator), layout density, AI tier, provider keys, and the active project folder. Guided shows two cards (Theme, AI mode); Operator exposes the full left nav: Display, AI, Sidebar, Project, Services. The command palette (`Ctrl+K`) is fuzzy search over panels, actions, docs, and your prompts; `g`-prefix chords jump directly to panels.

## How to use it

**Set theme/persona/density:**

1. Open Settings, **Display** section.
2. Pick Theme (Light/Dark/System), Mode (Guided/Operator), Density (Compact/Roomy). Each applies immediately.

**Wire up cloud AI:**

1. Go to **AI**, set Tier to On-device only, On-device with optional cloud, or Cloud-first.
2. Paste a key into OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google. The field is write-only; you get a **Connected** / **Not connected** badge back, never the key.
3. For local AI, hit **Detect Ollama** (probes `localhost:11434`). Connect LangSmith under **Eval tools** to trace and run a small dev eval.

**Navigate by keyboard:**

1. `Ctrl+K` opens the palette; type, `Enter` to select, `Esc` to dismiss.
2. Outside any input, press `g` then a letter to jump (e.g. `g s` -> Services). `?` opens the shortcuts sheet.

**Reorder the sidebar:** Settings -> **Sidebar**: drag to reorder, eye toggle to hide. Overview, Release, and Pipeline are locked visible. **Reset to default** reverts.

## Common pitfalls

> [!NOTE] Guided hides the advanced surfaces
> Provider keys, project folder, sidebar editor, and density only exist in Operator. In Guided, click **See full settings** in the About card (it flips persona to Operator) to reach them.

> [!WARNING] Chords are suppressed in inputs and while the palette is open
> The `g`-prefix jumps and `?` yield whenever focus is in an input/textarea/contenteditable or the palette is open, so they never clobber typed keystrokes. Blur the field first. The `g` buffer also self-clears after ~1.5s, so a slow second key drops the chord.

> [!TIP] Start over is non-destructive to credentials
> **Start over** clears persona, active project, and first-run flags, then reopens the welcome flow. Saved API keys and connections persist (they live outside browser storage). It is a two-click confirm.

## Reference

Global shortcuts:

- `Ctrl+K` - command palette
- `?` - shortcuts sheet
- `Esc` / `Enter` - dismiss / select in palette

`g`-prefix nav chords:

- `g o` Overview
- `g w` Workboard
- `g r` Repo
- `g a` Architecture
- `g p` Pipeline
- `g s` Services
- `g b` Library
- `g l` Release
- `g y` Observability
- `g c` Activity
- `g t` Walk through
- `g d` Docs
- `g ,` Settings

Provider key badges:

- **Connected** - a validated key is stored for that provider
- **Not connected** - no key set
